Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore on 2013-04-12 as a special purpose vehicle.
At incorporation on 2013-04-12, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd had two directors, including Dag Oivind Dvergsten.
From 2013-12-20 until the company was wound up, Dag Oivind Dvergsten was the sole director of Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd.
On 2013-04-12, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd had an issued share capital of SGD 1 comprising a single share held by First Marine Holdings Pte Ltd.

On or about 2013-12-22, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd allotted an additional 999 shares to various parties, resulting in shareholdings of 21% for First Marine Holdings Pte Ltd, 3% for Hege Anfindsen, 25% for Dag Oivind Dvergsten, and 51% for Treatmil Holdings Limited.
First Marine Holdings Pte Ltd was wholly owned by Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd.
Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd was wholly owned by Dag Dvergsten AS, a Norwegian company.
Dag Oivind Dvergsten was a director and sole shareholder of Dag Dvergsten AS and also a director of Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd.
Dag Dvergsten AS owned 36.5% of the shares in Treatmil Holdings Limited.
Treatmil Holdings Limited was the sole shareholder of Atlantic Marine Services Asia Pacific Limited, which previously owned Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd.
Dag Oivind Dvergsten was a director of Treatmil Holdings Limited.

From incorporation, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd did not open or operate any bank account in its own name and instead used a pre-existing DBS Bank account held by Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd to receive funds and make payments in connection with its affairs.

On 2013-09-30, Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd agreed to provide a loan of USD 3,000,000 to Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd at an interest rate of 6% per annum, with repayment due on 2016-12-31.
On 2013-10-17, a tranche of the USD 3,000,000 loan from Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd was disbursed to Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd.
On 2013-10-18, a further tranche of the USD 3,000,000 loan from Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd was disbursed to Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd.

On 2013-10-23, the remaining tranche of the USD 3,000,000 loan from Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd was disbursed to Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd, completing the USD 3,000,000 disbursement.

On 2013-12-11, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd entered into a Memorandum of Agreement and a Supplemental Agreement with GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE for the proposed purchase of the jack-up drilling rig Somnath for USD 215,000,000.
Under the Somnath purchase agreements dated 2013-12-11, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd was pay a first deposit of USD 15,000,000.
On 2013-12-23, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd obtained a bridging loan of USD 15,000,000 from Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd, with repayment due on 2014-12-27.

Clause 2.2 of the 2013-12-23 loan agreement with Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd stated that the loan monies were to be applied to finance the first deposit for the Somnath purchase and other expenses related to the Somnath and the 2013-12-11 Memorandum of Agreement.

On 2013-12-24, Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd queried Dag Oivind Dvergsten about a discrepancy between the named payee Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd and the DBS account name Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd and requested details of a bank account held in Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd's name.

In an email dated 2013-12-24, Dag Oivind Dvergsten informed Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd that DBS Bank could not process a new account for Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd in time and requested that the Symphony loan monies be sent to the Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd DBS account, adding that morning remittance would allow processing for the rig.

On 2013-12-26, Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd transferred USD 6,000,000, being the first tranche of the USD 15,000,000 loan, to the DBS Bank account of Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd used for Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd's affairs.

On 2013-12-01, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd entered into an agreement with Dag Dvergsten AS for provision of high level business services related to the acquisition and financing of the Somnath rig.

Under the 2013-12-01 management services arrangement, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd made payments to Dag Dvergsten AS in 2013, 2014, and 2015 totaling USD 1,880,800, comprising USD 830,800, USD 750,000, and USD 300,000 respectively.

A document dated 2013-12-01 recorded a loan agreement under which Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd granted a loan of USD 1,700,000 to Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd at 4% per annum interest, with repayment due on 2023-12-31.

A separate document dated 2014-11-01 also recorded a loan agreement under which Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd granted a loan of USD 1,700,000 to Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd at 4% per annum interest, with repayment due on 2023-12-31.

On 2014-11-14, a novation agreement was executed between Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd, and First Marine Holdings Pte Ltd, providing for a USD 1,700,000 loan from Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd to Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd in connection with novation of First Marine Holdings Pte Ltd's rights under an agreement with PT Harmoni Drilling Services.

On 2014-11-14, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd and Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd entered into a business services agreement under which Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd was to pay USD 50,000 per month to Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd as management fees, with such amounts to be offset against the USD 1,700,000 loan.

On 2013-12-15, Dag Oivind Dvergsten instructed Abraham Thomas, owner of TY Global LLC, to send invoices to the address of Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd, and Abraham Thomas asked what invoice amount he should issue.

On 2013-12-27, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd transferred a total of USD 3,250,000 from funds held in the Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd DBS account to TY Global LLC based on invoices describing an upfront brokerage commission of USD 2,472,500 and advisory and financial consulting fees of USD 777,500 for the Somnath acquisition.

On 2013-12-27, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd transferred USD 1,280,000 from the Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd DBS account to a bank account of Dag Dvergsten AS, described as a payment to Treatmil Holdings Limited.

A tripartite agreement dated 2013-12-20 between Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd, Treatmil Holdings Limited, and Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd stated that a USD 1,280,000 payment to Treatmil Holdings Limited would be treated as a partial repayment of the USD 3,000,000 loan from Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd.

A payment schedule attached to the 2013-12-20 tripartite agreement recorded a reduction of the USD 3,000,000 loan from Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd to USD 1,720,000; one version of the schedule was unsigned and another version bore a digital form of the initials of Alfred Schwegler, then director of Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd.
On 2013-12-30, TY Global LLC transferred USD 2,250,000 to AT Offshore LLC.
On 2014-01-03, AT Offshore LLC transferred USD 2,000,000 to Rocky Point International LLC, an entity wholly and ultimately controlled and owned by Dag Oivind Dvergsten.
As at 2013-12-31, the bank account of Rocky Point International LLC had a balance of approximately USD 1,919.22 before receiving funds from AT Offshore LLC.

On 2014-04-21, Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd emailed Dag Oivind Dvergsten after seeing a news article stating that the Somnath rig was no longer available for purchase and requested clarification of the status of Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd's rights.
On 2014-04-21, Dag Oivind Dvergsten informed Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd by email that Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd still had rights to purchase the Somnath rig.

On 2014-06-04, Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd verbally requested from Dag Oivind Dvergsten particulars of how the USD 6,000,000 first tranche of the Symphony loan had been used, and on the same day he provided a breakdown of items without supporting documents.

On 2014-06-06, solicitors for Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd requested from Dag Oivind Dvergsten all correspondence between Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd and GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE reflecting the status of the right to purchase the Somnath rig.
A letter dated 2014-04-28 from GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE to Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd amended the required first deposit for the Somnath purchase to USD 7,500,000.

A letter dated 2014-06-16 from GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE to Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd stated that Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd had not paid the required deposit by the stipulated deadline.
On 2014-06-17, Dag Oivind Dvergsten emailed Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd enclosing the 2014-06-16 letter from GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE regarding default in payment of the deposit.

On 2014-06-18, Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd requested from Dag Oivind Dvergsten all correspondence between Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd and GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE relating to the default in making the deposit and the right to purchase the Somnath rig.
On 2014-06-19, Dag Oivind Dvergsten provided Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd with various letters exchanged between Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd and GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE.

On 2014-06-20, during a telephone call with a representative of Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd, Dag Oivind Dvergsten confirmed that Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd had received USD 1,500,000 from GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE after the Somnath rig was sold to a third party.

In an email dated 2014-06-20, a representative of Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd informed Dag Oivind Dvergsten that a side letter dated 2013-12-11 between Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd and GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE had not been provided and requested a copy.
A side letter dated 2013-12-11 between Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd and GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE was referred to in correspondence concerning the Somnath transaction.

The Supplemental Agreement dated 2013-12-11 between Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd and GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE contained a clause stating that if the Somnath transaction could not be completed, GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE would pay Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd a commission of USD 1,500,000 upon sale of the rig to a third party.

On 2014-07-09, Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd submitted a proof of debt stating that Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd owed the full USD 3,000,000 loan plus interest, totaling USD 3,318,575.34.

On 2014-07-02, Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd served a Notice of Default and Acceleration on Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd and Treatmil Holdings Limited, stating that non-disclosure of correspondence with GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE constituted an event of default under the Symphony loan.

On 2014-08-01, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd entered into a settlement agreement with Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd and Treatmil Holdings Limited providing for repayment of the USD 6,000,000 first tranche of the Symphony loan by 2014-12-26.
For the financial year ending 2013-12-31, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd recorded no revenue.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the conduct of Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd's sole director in directing and authorising payments and loans to related entities and third parties, using funds borrowed for the Somnath rig acquisition, resulted in financial outcomes adverse to the company and its creditors.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors are generally manage company assets and borrowed funds in a manner consistent with the company's commercial objectives and to consider the financial position of the company and the interests of creditors when the company has significant liabilities and limited capital.
Party_Details.Application: Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd was incorporated with minimal paid-up capital and used a bank account of Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd for its transactions. It entered into agreements to purchase the Somnath rig and obtained loans totaling USD 18,000,000 from Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd and Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd, with the Symphony loan expressly linked to funding the first deposit and related expenses. Within days of receiving the first USD 6,000,000 tranche from Symphony, Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd, through the Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd account, made substantial payments to TY Global LLC and to a Dag Dvergsten AS account for Treatmil Holdings Limited, and entered into or relied on management and service agreements with Dag Dvergsten AS and Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd that generated significant fee and loan flows to those entities. The company did not operate its own bank account, had no recorded revenue for 2013, and its obligations under the Somnath purchase agreements and loan agreements remained while funds were transferred to related and third-party entities. Correspondence in 2014 shows that Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd sought details of the use of its loan funds and information about the status of the Somnath transaction after learning that the rig had been sold to a third party, and that Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd had received USD 1,500,000 from GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE under the Supplemental Agreement.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed the director's duties under section 157(1) of the Companies Act and the common law duty to act bona fide in the interests of the company, applying the objective test from Intraco and Ho Kang Peng. It concluded that an honest and intelligent director could not reasonably have regarded the four sets of transactions (payments to Dag Dvergsten AS, the loan and service arrangements with Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd, the payments to TY Global LLC, and the payment described as to Treatmil Holdings Limited) as being in the interests of Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd. The court found that there was no adequate documentary support for the management fees to Dag Dvergsten AS and that the 2013 fee covered work largely related to an earlier memorandum of agreement to which the plaintiff was not a party. It inferred that the 2015 management fee had in fact been paid based on the defendant's own accounting of the USD 10.5 million inflows and outflows. Regarding the Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd loan and business services agreement, the court held that the novation arrangement conferred disproportionate benefit on Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd without clear benefit to the plaintiff, and noted the defendant's own admission that the loan terms were not advantageous to the company. The court found that the loan balance had been reduced by set-offs inconsistent with the timing of the plaintiff's winding up, undermining the credibility of the asserted service performance. For the TY Global LLC payments, the court held that the director had departed from industry practice by paying a large brokerage commission and advisory fee upfront from buyer funds when the Somnath purchase was not secured and when the brokers were already engaged by the seller. The court rejected the alleged oral reinvestment arrangement as unsupported by contemporaneous documents and inconsistent with the subsequent flow of USD 2,000,000 to Rocky Point International LLC and its use to pay Rocky Point's outstanding obligations. For the Treatmil payment, the court preferred the evidence of the past and present directors of Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd that they were unaware of the tripartite agreement and payment schedule, and noted that the schedule bore a digital version of Alfred Schwegler's initials without his consent. The court concluded that the plaintiff did not obtain a corresponding reduction of its loan liability to Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd. On the duty to consider creditors' interests, the court held that by 2013-12-27 the company was in a parlous financial position: it had nominal capital, no revenue, and substantial loan liabilities, while significant sums had been transferred out to related and third-party entities. It applied the approach in Progen Engineering, Dynasty, and Parakou Shipping to find that the director was required to take creditors' interests into account and had not done so, particularly in relation to Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd and Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd. On conflicts, the court held that the director had placed himself in positions of conflict and engaged in self-dealing by signing and implementing agreements on behalf of both the plaintiff and related entities (Dag Dvergsten AS, Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd, Treatmil Holdings Limited) and by directing funds to Rocky Point International LLC. It held that disclosure under section 156 of the Companies Act did not displace the general law no-conflict and self-dealing rules and that there was no effective ratification by fully informed shareholders, as Treatmil Holdings Limited's board minutes predated its acquisition of a majority shareholding and did not reflect full disclosure of the material facts of the four transactions. The court therefore held that the liquidators had established breaches of directors' duties but that the evidential threshold for fraudulent trading under section 340(1) of the Companies Act was not met, and it assessed losses by reference to the amounts transferred under the four impugned transaction categories.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Dag Oivind Dvergsten

Facts.Text: Dag Oivind Dvergsten is an individual who incorporated Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd in Singapore as a special purpose vehicle.
On 2013-04-12, Dag Oivind Dvergsten became one of two directors of Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd.
From 2013-12-20 until the winding up of Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd, Dag Oivind Dvergsten was its sole director.
Dag Oivind Dvergsten was a director and sole shareholder of Dag Dvergsten AS, a Norwegian company.
Dag Oivind Dvergsten was a director of Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd, a Singapore-incorporated company.
Dag Oivind Dvergsten was a director of Treatmil Holdings Limited.
Dag Dvergsten AS owned 36.5% of the shares in Treatmil Holdings Limited.
Rocky Point International LLC was wholly and ultimately controlled and owned by Dag Oivind Dvergsten.

Dag Oivind Dvergsten arranged for Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd to use a DBS Bank account held by Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd for receiving funds and making payments related to Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd's activities.
Dag Oivind Dvergsten negotiated a USD 3,000,000 loan from Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd to Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd on 2013-09-30.

Dag Oivind Dvergsten negotiated and signed on behalf of Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd the 2013-12-11 Memorandum of Agreement and Supplemental Agreement with GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE for the purchase of the Somnath rig.
On 2013-12-23, Dag Oivind Dvergsten arranged for Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd to obtain a USD 15,000,000 bridging loan from Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd.

On 2013-12-24, Dag Oivind Dvergsten emailed Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd stating that DBS Bank could not open a new account for Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd in time and requested that the Symphony loan funds be sent to the Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd DBS account, indicating a preference for morning remittance to begin processing for the rig.

On 2013-12-01, Dag Oivind Dvergsten caused Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd to enter into a management services agreement with Dag Dvergsten AS for business services related to the Somnath acquisition and financing.

Following the 2013-12-01 management services agreement, Dag Oivind Dvergsten directed or authorised payments from Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd to Dag Dvergsten AS totaling USD 1,880,800 over 2013, 2014, and 2015.
In December 2014, Dag Oivind Dvergsten arranged for Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd to enter into a further management services agreement with Dag Dvergsten AS for services in 2014.

Dag Oivind Dvergsten signed on behalf of Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd and Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd documents dated 2013-12-01 and 2014-11-01 recording a USD 1,700,000 loan from Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd to Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd at 4% interest with repayment due on 2023-12-31.

On 2014-11-14, Dag Oivind Dvergsten signed a novation agreement on behalf of First Marine Holdings Pte Ltd, Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd, and Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd, providing for a USD 1,700,000 loan from Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd to Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd and for offsets of management services and outlays against that loan.

On 2014-11-14, Dag Oivind Dvergsten signed a business services agreement on behalf of Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd and Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd under which Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd was to pay USD 50,000 per month to Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd as management fees, to be offset against the USD 1,700,000 loan.
On 2013-12-15, Dag Oivind Dvergsten instructed Abraham Thomas of TY Global LLC to issue invoices to Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd, and Abraham Thomas requested the invoice amounts.

On 2013-12-27, Dag Oivind Dvergsten directed or authorised transfers totaling USD 3,250,000 from funds of Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd held in the Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd DBS account to TY Global LLC based on invoices for brokerage commission and advisory and financial consulting related to the Somnath rig.

On 2013-12-27, Dag Oivind Dvergsten directed or authorised a transfer of USD 1,280,000 from the Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd DBS account to a Dag Dvergsten AS bank account, described as a payment to Treatmil Holdings Limited.

Dag Oivind Dvergsten signed a tripartite agreement dated 2013-12-20 on behalf of Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd and Treatmil Holdings Limited, which described the USD 1,280,000 payment as a partial repayment of the Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd loan.

On 2014-04-21, after Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd queried a news article about the Somnath rig, Dag Oivind Dvergsten informed Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd that Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd still had rights to purchase the rig.

On 2014-06-04, Dag Oivind Dvergsten provided Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd with a breakdown of how the USD 6,000,000 first tranche of the Symphony loan had been used, without attaching supporting documents.

On 2014-06-17, Dag Oivind Dvergsten emailed Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd a letter from GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE dated 2014-06-16 stating that Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd had not paid the required deposit by the deadline.

On 2014-06-20, in a telephone call with a representative of Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd, Dag Oivind Dvergsten confirmed that Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd had received USD 1,500,000 from GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE after the Somnath rig was sold to a third party.

On 2014-06-20, a representative of Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd emailed Dag Oivind Dvergsten noting that a side letter dated 2013-12-11 between Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd and GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE had not been provided and requested a copy; Dag Oivind Dvergsten did not respond with the side letter.

On 2013-04-12, Dag Oivind Dvergsten provided written notice to the board of Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd of his interests in Dag Dvergsten AS, Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd, Treatmil Holdings Limited, and Rocky Point International LLC.

On 2013-12-03, the board of Treatmil Holdings Limited held a meeting at which it considered subscribing for a 51% shareholding in Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd and discussed the Somnath rig project and related financing structure.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-dispute conduct of Dag Oivind Dvergsten in structuring and directing the use of Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd's borrowed funds, including payments and loans to entities he controlled or was associated with, corresponded with the eventual judicial determination of his responsibility for the financial consequences to the company and its creditors.
Party_Details.Rule: A director who controls the financial decisions of a company is generally assessed against standards requiring that company funds, especially borrowed funds, be applied consistently with the stated purposes of the borrowing and with regard to the company's financial position and the interests of creditors when the company has substantial liabilities and limited capital.
Party_Details.Application: Dag Oivind Dvergsten incorporated Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd as a special purpose vehicle and served as its sole director from 2013-12-20. He also controlled or held key roles in Dag Dvergsten AS, Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd, Treatmil Holdings Limited, and Rocky Point International LLC. Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd obtained a USD 3,000,000 loan from Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd and a USD 15,000,000 bridging loan from Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd, with the Symphony loan expressly linked to funding the first deposit for the Somnath rig and related expenses. Instead of opening a bank account in the company's name, he used a Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd DBS account for all inflows and outflows. Within days of receiving the first USD 6,000,000 tranche from Symphony, he directed or authorised transfers totaling USD 3,250,000 to TY Global LLC and USD 1,280,000 to a Dag Dvergsten AS account for Treatmil Holdings Limited, and he had already put in place or later documented management and service agreements under which Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd paid or was recorded as owing substantial fees to Dag Dvergsten AS and Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd. Funds sent to TY Global LLC were largely passed through to AT Offshore LLC and then to Rocky Point International LLC, which he owned and controlled, and were used to pay Rocky Point International LLC's outstanding obligations. He also signed a tripartite agreement and payment schedule describing the USD 1,280,000 payment as a partial repayment of the Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd loan, while later correspondence from Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd's directors indicated that they were unaware of this arrangement. In 2014, when Symphony Ventures Pte Ltd learned that the Somnath rig had been sold to a third party, it sought details of the use of its loan funds and the status of the transaction, and Dag Oivind Dvergsten provided a breakdown without supporting documents and confirmed receipt of USD 1,500,000 from GOL Offshore Fujairah LLC FZE. These pre-dispute actions formed the factual basis for the court's later assessment of his responsibility for the company's inability to meet its obligations and for the loss of funds applied to related and third-party entities.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court examined Dag Oivind Dvergsten's role as sole director and his control over related entities, and applied the objective standard for directors' duties under section 157(1) of the Companies Act and the common law. It found that he had caused Traxiar Drilling Partners II Pte Ltd to enter into and implement four categories of transactions that were not in the company's interests: management fee payments to Dag Dvergsten AS, the USD 1,700,000 loan and associated business services agreement with Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd, the USD 3,250,000 payments to TY Global LLC, and the USD 1,280,000 payment described as to Treatmil Holdings Limited. The court held that there was no adequate evidence of services justifying the management fees to Dag Dvergsten AS and that the 2013 fee related largely to work on an earlier memorandum of agreement to which the plaintiff was not a party. It concluded that the 2015 management fee had effectively been paid based on the defendant's own accounting of the USD 10.5 million inflows and outflows. Regarding the Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd loan and business services agreement, the court held that the novation arrangement conferred disproportionate benefit on Dag Dvergsten Pte Ltd without clear benefit to the plaintiff, and noted the defendant's admission that the loan terms were not advantageous to the company. It found that the loan balance had been reduced by set-offs inconsistent with the timing of the plaintiff's winding up, undermining the credibility of the asserted service performance. For the TY Global LLC payments, the court held that the defendant had departed from industry practice by paying a large brokerage commission and advisory fee upfront from buyer funds when the Somnath purchase was not secured and when the brokers were already engaged by the seller. The court rejected the alleged oral reinvestment arrangement as unsupported by contemporaneous documents and inconsistent with the subsequent flow of USD 2,000,000 to Rocky Point International LLC and its use to pay Rocky Point International LLC's outstanding obligations. For the Treatmil payment, the court preferred the evidence of the past and present directors of Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd that they were unaware of the tripartite agreement and payment schedule, and noted that the schedule bore a digital version of Alfred Schwegler's initials without his consent. The court concluded that the plaintiff did not obtain a corresponding reduction of its loan liability to Atlantic Marine Services Singapore Pte Ltd. On the duty to consider creditors' interests, the court held that by 2013-12-27 the company was in a parlous financial position and that the defendant was required to take creditors' interests into account but instead directed substantial funds to related and third-party entities, including his own companies, rather than applying them to the Somnath acquisition or to repayment of loans. On conflicts, the court held that he had placed himself in positions of conflict and engaged in self-dealing by signing and implementing agreements on behalf of both the plaintiff and related entities and by directing funds to Rocky Point International LLC. It held that disclosure under section 156 of the Companies Act did not displace the general law no-conflict and self-dealing rules and that there was no effective ratification by fully informed shareholders. The court therefore held him liable for breaches of directors' duties and ordered him to account for losses associated with the four categories of transactions, while declining to find that the standard for fraudulent trading under section 340(1) of the Companies Act had been met.
